summary: I was born in Oklahoma on February 19, 1926, and I have lived through many changes here. My family and I have strong ties to the state, especially through education and community life. Oklahoma has been the backdrop for much of my personal and professional journey, including my work in schools and my father's role at Oklahoma State University.

Currently, I live in my home at Bolewood Acres in Tulsa, Oklahoma. This place has been my residence for many years, and it’s where I’m recording this interview with my wife Martha. Bolewood Acres feels like a comfortable and familiar place to reflect on my life and experiences.

Tulsa has been an important city in my life, especially in my career as an educator. I worked at Whitney Junior High here before moving to Jenks, and now I live in Bolewood Acres, Tulsa. It’s where I met my wife Martha and where much of my adult life has unfolded.

After earning my master’s degree at Oklahoma State University, I worked as a reading diagnostician in Stillwater. I was employed at the high school there, which was an important early step in my educational career. Stillwater was where I began to develop my professional skills before moving on to other school districts.

Oklahoma State University played a big role in my life and my family’s. My father worked there as the director of placement, and I earned my master's degree from OSU. It was also the place where I found job opportunities through my dad’s connections, which helped me move from Whitney Junior High to Jenks grade schools.

I worked as a reading diagnostician in the Jenks grade school system, which was my last position before retiring. I moved there after working at Whitney Junior High in Tulsa, thanks to a job opening my dad told me about. Jenks is also where I met my husband, making it a special place in my personal and professional life.

I taught at Whitney Junior High School in Tulsa, which was located behind Hale High School. Unfortunately, the school lost its funding, which led me to seek other employment. Whitney Junior High was an important part of my teaching career and helped shape my experience as an educator.

My brother was very fortunate to attend the Massachusetts Institute of Technology on a National Merit scholarship. He majored in physics there, which was a big achievement for our family. Our dad always encouraged us to pursue scholarships so we could go wherever we wanted, and MIT was where my brother ended up.

I was born in Carthage, Missouri, but we only lived there for about six months before moving to California. My early memories are limited because I was less than a year old when we left. Carthage was the starting point of my life before my family sought better opportunities elsewhere.

My family moved to Fresno, California, when I was a baby because my father was a barber and there was a good demand for that work there. We were happy for a time, but my father’s drinking caused problems, and eventually my mother decided to move back to Joplin, Missouri. Fresno was where I spent my earliest years before returning to Missouri.

I learned that Frank Phillips was a barber early in his career, which is interesting because my father was also a barber. This connection came up when talking about my family's history and the barber business. Though I didn’t know him personally, his story relates to my father’s profession and the era we lived in.

After leaving California, my mother and I moved back to Joplin, Missouri, to live with my grandmother. I attended Columbia Grade School and then North Junior High School there. Joplin was where I grew up, went to school, and experienced many formative events during my childhood and adolescence.

One of my brothers worked for the Civilian Conservation Corps when he was about fifteen or sixteen. The CCC was one of the government programs that helped young men find work during that time. It was part of the broader context of the war and economic challenges we faced as a family.

My brother Bill served in the United States Marine Corps and was stationed at Hickam Field during the attack on December 7, 1941. He was very lucky not to be hurt during that event. His service was a source of concern for our family, especially my mother, during those tense times.

Hickam Field was where my brother Bill was stationed in the Marine Corps on December 7, 1941, when the Japanese attacked Pearl Harbor. He was fortunate to survive without injury. Knowing he was there made my mother very nervous during those early days of the war.

I vividly remember December 7, 1941, the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. I was about fifteen and was outside with a friend when my mother came running out, terrified for my brothers who were in the service. It was a moment that changed everything and brought the war very close to home.

My mother sold Fashion Frocks in Joplin for about three or four years during tough times. It was like selling dress patterns, where she would take orders and people would make the dresses. Though it didn’t bring in much money, it was one way she helped support our family.

My grandmother worked at Crispen's Dry Goods, located at 6th and Main Street in Joplin. She would bring home scraps of wool and suede from work and sew them into clothes for us. This resourcefulness helped our family get by during hard times.

Crispen's Dry Goods, where my grandmother worked, was located at 6th and Main Street in Joplin. This area was part of my childhood environment and where my family found ways to make ends meet. The store and its scraps became a source of material for homemade clothing.

I attended Columbia Grade School in Joplin for six years, which was about six or seven blocks from my grandmother’s house. It was my primary school and where I spent much of my early education. The proximity made it convenient and a familiar part of my childhood.

After Columbia Grade School, I went to North Junior High School in Joplin and was very happy there. It covered grades seven through nine, and I developed a strong interest in chemistry during that time. The teachers were supportive, and I even helped grade papers in my senior year of high school.

In 1944, right after graduating from high school, I was sent to Leavenworth, Kansas, to be inducted into the United States Army. It was a big transition from civilian life to military service. Leavenworth was the place where we were examined and processed before training.

I enlisted in the United States Army during World War II after being drafted. My prior ROTC experience in high school helped me adjust quickly. I trained at Camp Barkeley in Texas and learned about weapons and military life alongside many other young men.

Camp Barkeley in Texas was where I was stationed for basic training after joining the Army. It was there that we learned about weapons, including grenades, and military discipline. The training was intense but necessary preparation for service during the war.

One of my brothers, Wesley, was stationed with the Army in the Aleutian Islands during World War II. There weren’t many soldiers there, so his presence was significant. My mother was very worried about him and my other brother who was in the Marines at the time.

My brother Bill was in the United States Marine Corps and was stationed at Camp Pendleton during the war. He was lucky to survive the attack on Pearl Harbor without injury. Camp Pendleton was a key part of his military service and our family’s wartime experience.
